hq: Not disclosed (address field left blank in report)
signing_authority: Not completed — management assertion signature block left as placeholder ('Your Name Here') with yellow highlight

system_description.overview: Vocca is an AI receptionist platform for medical clinics and practices that handles inbound and outbound calls and automates admin tasks for healthcare centers. The 3-month audit period (May–August 2025) is unusually short for a Type 2 report.

products.Vocca AI Receptionist: AI-powered inbound/outbound call handling and admin automation for healthcare clinics and practices
products.NOTE_BOILERPLATE_CONTAMINATION: The system description body (pages 13–15) was NOT customized — it describes a generic Accorp boilerplate 'AI-automated compliance platform' for SOC 2/HIPAA/ISO 27001/GDPR/PCI DSS with yellow-highlighted placeholder instructions still visible in the published PDF. The actual product description (AI receptionist for healthcare) only appears in the auditor scope and management assertion sections.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Supabase (carve-out subservice organization — Supabase has its own SOC 2)
regions: Not specified — 'VPC WEST' and 'VPC EAST' shown in sample/placeholder network diagram
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — 'Vocca does not have any physical servers and all data is processed on cloud services'
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — 'high availability data centers with multiple availability zones'; DR plan covers 'spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in Supabase'

network_architecture.network_segmentation: VPC environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls on application gateway and production network; firewall rules reviewed annually. Network firewalls implemented to control incoming/outgoing traffic based on predetermined security rules.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via Supabase to identify and protect against threats
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN (IPSec VPN tunnel shown in placeholder diagram)
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet with content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> VPC VPN Connection -> VPC WEST / VPC EAST (with Internet Gateways and multiple Availability Zones) -> private VPC subnets (Prod HA, Dev, Test, D.con) -> VPN Gateway -> IPSec VPN Tunnel -> Controllers. NOTE: diagram in report is an Accorp sample/placeholder, not Vocca's actual network diagram.

application_architecture.environments: Separate development, test, staging, and production environments mentioned in change management controls

data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily — 'Backups are taken daily, multi-availability zones are configured'

authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code repository access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Managed via Supabase password policy — includes periodic forced changes, expiry, and complexity requirements. Accounts locked after limited unsuccessful logon attempts.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider KMS (Supabase) — 'organization uses its cloud provider key management service to encrypt data at rest and to store and manage encryption keys'

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named (generic 'source code management tool' referenced throughout)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to production; developers cannot make production changes without additional approval
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained as code with configuration management tool for implementation and rollback
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: CTO/Management sets up automatic patch management; monthly patch management reviews; workstations auto-update and scanned daily for patch compliance; OS patches managed automatically on renewal for network devices

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named — generic 'monitoring tool' with threshold-based alerting
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, roles. Access to modify logs is restricted.
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts for high and critical risk events via incident management/ticketing system

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud/data center subservice organization — database, networking, VPC, physical infrastructure, IDS/IPS

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and malware protection on all production servers (continuous auto-scan); definitions updated weekly on production servers, daily on workstations. Also installed on Mac workstations/laptops.
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; internal IT team performs periodic scans per audit calendar
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant personnel/role changes during engagement period (control could not be tested)
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance control untestable because no cyber incidents reported during engagement period
CC6.5.2 — Customer data deletion upon termination untestable because no customer terminations during engagement period
CC7.3.1 — Security incident response untestable because no security incidents reported during engagement period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access to facilities excluded; Supabase is responsible for physical security of its data center
A1.2 — Availability environmental protections excluded; Vocca does not have an office and all environmental security controls managed by building management/Supabase
notable_controls: CC7.4.2 (CC7.2) — Control CC7.4.2 confirms backups are taken daily with multi-AZ configuration
CC8.1.2 — Branch protection rules enforced for all merges to master/production branch
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration testing by independent third party with high/critical findings tracked via incident management

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-AZ on Supabase with VPC, IDS/IPS, VPN. However: single vendor (Supabase only, carve-out), no CDN, no named regions, no IaC beyond baseline config management. Entirely dependent on Supabase infrastructure which is carved out of scope.

application_architecture.rationale: Near-zero disclosure. No languages, frameworks, compute type, or API style mentioned. Branch protection and separate environments confirmed but almost nothing else about the application stack is disclosed.

data_layer.rationale: Daily backups, multi-AZ, data classification and retention policies confirmed, per-tenant segregation mentioned. However: only Supabase database mentioned, no caching layer, no data warehouse, no specifics on backup testing or restore validation.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS, MFA on admin/prod access, antivirus, cybersecurity insurance, firewall rules, server hardening — good baseline. Negatives: no WAF mentioned, no SIEM named, no named security tooling, 3 controls untestable due to no incidents (short audit window concern).

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection, separate environments, emergency change process, IaC for baseline configs, automatic patch management confirmed. However: no CI/CD tool named, no source control tool named, no container orchestration, no named deployment tooling.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and annually reviewed, tested annually, daily backups with multi-AZ, DR plan covers AZ failover within Supabase. Negatives: no RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, entirely dependent on Supabase AZ availability.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Extreme single-vendor concentration: Supabase is the only named third-party vendor and is responsible for all infrastructure, database, networking, physical security, IDS/IPS, and password policy. No other cloud, CDN, or technology vendors named anywhere in the report.

overall.rationale: This is a very early-stage SOC 2 with significant concerns: (1) system description section was not customized and still contains Accorp boilerplate placeholder text with visible instructions; (2) management assertion signature block left blank; (3) network diagram is a sample placeholder not replaced with actual architecture; (4) only 3-month audit window; (5) near-total infrastructure dependence on single carved-out vendor (Supabase); (6) no named tech stack, CI/CD, monitoring, or security tooling. Controls pass (0 exceptions) but the report quality and disclosure depth are very low.

red_flags: CRITICAL: System description (pages 13-15) contains Accorp boilerplate placeholder text that was NOT replaced — describes a generic 'AI-automated compliance platform for SOC 2/HIPAA/GDPR/PCI DSS' rather than Vocca's actual AI receptionist product. Yellow-highlighted instructions to modify the text are still visible in the published PDF.
CRITICAL: Management assertion signature block is blank — 'Your Name Here' placeholder with yellow highlight, no actual signing authority name or designation recorded.
Network diagram on page 19 is an Accorp sample placeholder with explicit instructions to replace it with actual network topology. Vocca never replaced it.
Only 3-month audit period (May 1 – August 1, 2025) — unusually short for SOC 2 Type 2; significantly limits value of operating effectiveness opinion.

Extreme single-vendor dependency: entire production infrastructure, database, networking, physical security, IDS/IPS, and password management all routed through Supabase (which is carved out of scope).
No RTO or RPO disclosed despite BCDR policy existing.
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded — significant gap for a healthcare-adjacent AI platform handling patient call data.
No WAF mentioned despite internet-facing application.
yellow_flags: Trust criteria limited to Security only — Availability and Confidentiality are also not in scope, which is unusual for a platform handling healthcare call data.
No named CI/CD tooling, monitoring platform, SIEM, or APM tool — all described generically.
No named source control system (GitHub/GitLab etc.) despite controls referencing it extensively.
3 controls untestable due to no incidents in the period — partially attributable to the short 3-month window.
No cybersecurity insurance incident could be tested (no cyber incidents in period) — also attributable to short window.
HQ address field blank in the company metadata.
green_flags: Unqualified opinion with 0 exceptions across all 57 controls tested.
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party with formal remediation tracking.
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plans for high/critical findings.
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts.
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration and automated alerts.
Branch protection rules enforced — developers cannot push to production without additional approval.
MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access.
Access revocation within one business day of termination with formal checklist.
Supabase holds its own SOC 2 report, which Vocca reviews annually.
key_observations: This appears to be Vocca's first SOC 2 report — the Accorp template was largely not customized, strongly suggesting the report was assembled quickly with minimal company-specific content. The 3-month period reinforces this being an inaugural audit.

The system description mismatch (compliance platform vs. AI receptionist) is a material quality issue — it raises questions about how thoroughly management reviewed the report before signing (or in this case, not signing).

Supabase as the sole named vendor is interesting for a healthcare AI platform. Supabase is a relatively young open-source infrastructure company and may not have the enterprise security posture expected for HIPAA-adjacent workloads.

The absence of Availability and Confidentiality criteria is notable for a company that handles medical clinic call recordings and patient appointment data — these would typically be in scope for healthcare tech.

The report contains no mention of HIPAA compliance, BAA agreements, or PHI handling controls despite the company explicitly serving medical clinics — this is a significant gap for a healthcare-adjacent product.

The AI/ML stack (which would be core to an AI receptionist product) receives zero technical description — no LLM provider, no telephony platform (Twilio/Telnyx/etc.), no speech-to-text service mentioned anywhere in the report.
